title: Image-Well Solution for Island Aquifers with Pumping, Recharge, and Complex Coastlines

research.question: Can an analytical image-well framework retain computational efficiency while representing transient pumping, recharge, multiple wells, and an irregular island coastline?
research.system: A homogeneous isotropic unconfined island aquifer with a sharp freshwater-saltwater interface, Dupuit flow, steady recharge, prescribed ocean head, and one or more pumping wells.
research.inputData: An analytical pumping-recharge solution, coastline boundary nodes derived from a GIS outline of Kinmen Island, hydraulic properties from literature, pumping and recharge perturbations, and nine illustrative pumping-well locations.
research.method: The framework linearizes the head equation with constant mean saturated thickness, superposes recharge, pumping, and initial-head components, solves image-well strengths at coastline nodes, derives interface-depth vulnerability and first-order reliability indices, and embeds the solution in constrained pumping optimization.
research.benchmark: Convergence is tested for a circular island against Kurylyk et al. (2024) while boundary-node density varies from 0.1 to 2 nodes per meter; the irregular Kinmen geometry is also compared with its best-fit circular approximation.
research.uncertainty: Reliability indices propagate specified pumping and recharge parameter variances with first-order variance estimation; model-form uncertainty, data availability, heterogeneity, anisotropy, tides, and transition-zone width are excluded.
research.conclusion: The image-well solution converges toward the circular-island benchmark, captures coastline-driven head and interface differences missed by a circular approximation, and rapidly supports vulnerability analysis and multiwell pumping optimization as a preliminary coastal-aquifer tool.

narrative.throughline: An island's real coastline changes the freshwater lens, so the ocean boundary must enter the analytical solution without giving up the speed needed for management calculations.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: The audience understands that fresh groundwater forms a lens above denser seawater and that pumping can pull the interface upward toward a well.
narrative.stakes: Replacing an irregular island with a circle can shift predicted interface depth enough to change pumping and saltwater-intrusion decisions.
narrative.resolution: Discretized coastline nodes become image wells whose solved strengths enforce ocean head around the actual island, allowing rapid maps of head, vulnerability, reliability, and feasible pumping.

background.premise: Small-island freshwater lenses are limited water stores that are vulnerable to pumping, drought, sea-level rise, erosion, and land-use change.
background.visibleEvidence: A lenticular freshwater body floats above saline groundwater while a well and falling recharge visibly thin the lens.
background.whyNow: The viewer must first see the physical resource whose geometry and safe yield the model is intended to protect.
background.boundary: The paper treats sharp-interface freshwater lenses rather than density-dependent mixing zones.
background.sourceExcerpt: These lenses provide critical water resources for island
background.visualObject: Island cross-section with a lenticular freshwater lens, saline water, recharge, and a pumping well
background.motionPurpose: Lower the water table near the well so the amplified interface rise becomes physically legible.
background.stepDetail: Draw the island and sea; reveal the fresh lens; add recharge; start pumping; show local lens thinning.
background.transitionBridge: The lens cross-section rotates into a plan-view coastline.

background.premise: Existing analytical freshwater-lens solutions usually assume idealized island geometry and simplified boundaries that do not represent complex coastlines or multiple stressors.
background.visibleEvidence: An idealized smooth boundary is replaced by a complex island coastline that requires explicit boundary representation.
background.whyNow: The research gap is geometric: the analytical speed is useful, but the idealized boundary can change the management result.
background.boundary: The paper does not claim numerical-model detail; it seeks a more flexible analytical approximation.
background.locator: PDF page 1, Abstract and Section 1 Introduction
background.sourceExcerpt: Past analytical solutions often rely on simplified boundary conditions and
background.visualObject: Idealized smooth island boundary beside a complex irregular coastline
background.motionPurpose: Replace the idealized boundary with a complex shoreline so the analytical geometry gap is visible.
background.stepDetail: Show the idealized boundary; retain the island interior; reveal shoreline indentations and protrusions; mark the boundary-representation gap.
background.transitionBridge: Points appear along the irregular shoreline where the ocean condition must be enforced.

background.premise: The sharp-interface Ghyben-Herzberg and Dupuit representation assumes stagnant uniform-salinity seawater and equilibrium between water-table elevation and interface depth.
background.visibleEvidence: A vertical water-table displacement maps through a sharp interface to a much larger opposing depth displacement.
background.whyNow: The analytical geometry-to-interface conversion must be visible before any head result is interpreted as saltwater-intrusion risk.
background.boundary: Finite transition-zone width, vertical flow, and mobile saline groundwater are outside the model.
background.locator: PDF page 2, Section 2.1 Fundamental solution, paragraph following Equation 2
background.sourceExcerpt: island aquifer contains stagnant (immobile) groundwater of uniform salinity, typically
background.visualObject: Sharp freshwater-saltwater interface linked vertically to the water table
background.motionPurpose: Connect a small head change to the larger interface response implied by density balance.
background.stepDetail: Mark sea level; mark water-table elevation; reveal the sharp interface; couple their vertical motions.
background.transitionBridge: The linked head and interface become the state predicted by the analytical solution.

methods.input: Nonlinear unconfined flow with time-varying saturated thickness
methods.validity: The paper limits the approximation to cases where saturated thickness varies by no more than approximately 50%.
methods.locator: PDF page 2, Section 2.1, discussion of Equations 1a-1c
methods.sourceExcerpt: thickness does not vary by more than approximately 50% throughout
methods.visualObject: Variable lens thickness settling around one mean-thickness plane
methods.motionPurpose: Replace a changing coefficient with a fixed reference thickness so superposition becomes available.
methods.stepDetail: Show the varying saturated thickness; mark its mean; freeze the coefficient at that mean; expose the linear operator.
methods.transitionBridge: The linear operator separates into recharge, pumping, and initial-head responses.

methods.input: Separate recharge, pumping, and initial-head responses
methods.output: A fundamental hydraulic-head solution without the ocean boundary
methods.validity: Superposition is valid for the linearized governing equation and preserves the contribution of each stress.
methods.locator: PDF page 2, Section 2.1, Equations 6-7; PDF page 9, Appendix A
methods.sourceExcerpt: The analytical solution of Eq. (1a) consists of three components:
methods.visualObject: Recharge mound, pumping cone, and initial-head plane stacking into one head surface
methods.motionPurpose: Add physically distinct head components while keeping their geometric contributions traceable.
methods.stepDetail: Reveal the initial plane; add the recharge mound; subtract the pumping cone; retain the composite head surface.
methods.transitionBridge: The composite surface meets the unsatisfied shoreline nodes.

methods.input: Latitude-longitude vertices from the Kinmen GIS shoreline
methods.output: Planar shoreline coordinates in a projected coordinate system
methods.validity: The source projects the shapefile vertices into a planar system such as UTM before perimeter distances are sampled.
methods.sourceExcerpt: shoreline, which are projected into a planar coordinate system (e.g., UTM) to obtain
methods.visualObject: Latitude-longitude shoreline vertices flattening onto a planar Kinmen coordinate grid
methods.motionPurpose: Convert geographic shoreline vertices into planar coordinates before distance-based sampling.
methods.stepDetail: Retain the GIS vertices; expose the geographic grid; apply the planar projection; label the resulting x-y shoreline coordinates.
methods.transitionBridge: The projected polygon perimeter becomes the distance track for boundary-node sampling.

methods.input: Projected planar coordinates along the irregular island shoreline
methods.output: A boundary-node set at user-defined spacing
methods.validity: Sampling the polygon perimeter preserves coastline features at a controllable spatial resolution.
methods.sourceExcerpt: user-defined intervals along the polygon boundary to generate the boundary-node
methods.visualObject: Kinmen shoreline acquiring evenly spaced boundary nodes
methods.motionPurpose: Convert a continuous irregular boundary into the finite locations needed by the image-well system.
methods.stepDetail: Retain the planar shoreline; trace cumulative boundary distance; place nodes at the selected interval.
methods.transitionBridge: Each boundary node emits an unknown image-well strength.

methods.input: Fundamental head values and unknown image-well strengths at coastline nodes
methods.output: Image-well weights that enforce prescribed ocean head
methods.validity: Substituting the composite solution at every node produces a square linear system under the linearized flow equation.
methods.locator: PDF page 9, Appendix B, Equations B.1-B.5
methods.sourceExcerpt: a system of linear equations derived from the boundary conditions.
methods.visualObject: Boundary-node residual bars collapsing to zero as image-well weights settle
methods.motionPurpose: Adjust artificial source and sink strengths until every shoreline node matches ocean head.
methods.stepDetail: Evaluate head at each node; assemble the coefficient matrix; solve for weights; drive all boundary residuals to zero.
methods.transitionBridge: The solved image-well weights enter the composite solution at interior locations.

methods.input: Fundamental response and solved image-well weights evaluated at island interior coordinates
methods.output: Hydraulic-head values throughout the irregular island domain
methods.validity: Equation 8a evaluates the composite pumping recharge initial-head and image-well response at a specified interior location after the boundary weights are solved.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.2 Image-well method, Equation 8a and following definitions
methods.visualObject: Solved coastline image wells emitting contributions that sum at successive interior grid points
methods.motionPurpose: Turn boundary enforcement into the interior head field used by every later interface calculation.
methods.stepDetail: Retain the solved weights; select an interior coordinate; sum the fundamental and image-well contributions; repeat across the island grid.
methods.transitionBridge: The evaluated interior head field becomes the input to the freshwater-interface relation.

methods.input: Hydraulic-head field above reference sea level
methods.validity: The conversion follows the sharp-interface Ghyben-Herzberg relation under the stated density and Dupuit assumptions.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Dupuit approximation, which creates equilibrium between the water table elevation
methods.visualObject: Plan-view head surface extruding downward into a lens cross-section
methods.motionPurpose: Turn the modeled head into the management object: interface depth beneath each point.
methods.stepDetail: Select a head contour; project it to a cross-section; apply the density relation; reveal the corresponding interface.
methods.transitionBridge: The interface depth becomes the quantity perturbed by pumping and recharge.

methods.input: Interface depth under a baseline pumping or recharge parameter
methods.output: Normalized vulnerability index for that parameter
methods.validity: A forward finite difference approximates the local interface-depth sensitivity with a prescribed small parameter perturbation.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.3 Vulnerability indices, Equations 10-11
methods.sourceExcerpt: complex, Eq. (10) can be easily calculated using the finite-difference
methods.visualObject: Baseline and perturbed interface surfaces separated by a local sensitivity band
methods.motionPurpose: Make pumping sensitivity local and recharge sensitivity distributed by comparing two nearby states.
methods.stepDetail: Hold the baseline interface; perturb one parameter; compute the depth difference; normalize the sensitivity field.
methods.transitionBridge: The sensitivity field receives parameter-uncertainty widths.

methods.validity: First-order variance estimation attributes output variability to pumping- or recharge-parameter uncertainty.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.4 Reliability indices, Equations 12-13
methods.sourceExcerpt: Reliability indices are based on uncertainty analysis using a first-
methods.visualObject: Vulnerability map overlaid with uncertainty-width contours
methods.motionPurpose: Separate high sensitivity from high uncertainty so monitoring priorities become visible.
methods.stepDetail: Attach a variance to each driver; multiply by local sensitivity; assemble output variance; map each contribution.
methods.transitionBridge: The reliability field fades into the circular-island verification geometry used for controlled refinement.

methods.input: Circular-island boundary systems spanning the prescribed node-density range
methods.output: Circular-island head solutions at each node density
methods.validity: Each controlled node density determines one boundary-node count and one linear system that is solved before comparison.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.5 Model verification and convergence analysis
methods.visualObject: Progressively denser circular boundary nodes feeding a sequence of solved head profiles
methods.motionPurpose: Solve each refinement level so later accuracy and runtime comparisons use the same controlled solution series.
methods.stepDetail: Set one node density; assemble its boundary system; solve its head profile; repeat through the prescribed density range.
methods.transitionBridge: The refined solution series aligns with the circular-island reference for an error comparison.

methods.input: Refined image-well head profiles and the circular-island analytical reference
methods.output: A convergence trend from approximately 0.356 to 0.070 error
methods.validity: Kurylyk et al. (2024) supplies the steady-state reference for the same simplified circular geometry and stresses.
methods.sourceExcerpt: reduces the average relative error (from approximately 0.356 down
methods.visualObject: Refined radial head profiles converging toward one circular-island reference profile
methods.motionPurpose: Measure solution agreement against the same reference as node density increases.
methods.stepDetail: Retain the reference profile; overlay each refined profile; compute its average relative error; order the errors by node density.
methods.transitionBridge: The same refinement series enters a dedicated runtime measurement.

methods.input: Solved image-well boundary systems at each controlled node density on the reported laptop
methods.output: One measured execution time for every refinement level
methods.validity: The source reports execution times for the same node-density sequence on an Intel Core i5-8265U laptop with 8 GB RAM.
methods.sourceExcerpt: it keeps the error below 0.1 while maintaining a runtime near 7.4 s
methods.visualObject: Each solved boundary system receives a separate stopwatch reading on one fixed laptop configuration
methods.motionPurpose: Produce the runtime series before comparing computational cost across refinement levels.
methods.stepDetail: Retain each solved system; start the fixed execution clock; record its completion time; attach the reading to its node density.
methods.transitionBridge: The measured runtime series moves onto a common refinement axis.

methods.input: Measured image-well execution times across the controlled boundary-node densities
methods.output: Runtime growth from about 0.19 seconds to 28.7 seconds
methods.validity: The reported timings use the stated standard-laptop configuration and the same refinement sequence.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 3.1 Verification and convergence analysis
methods.sourceExcerpt: increases the computational time (from about 0.19 s
methods.visualObject: Boundary-system sizes aligned with a separate rising runtime scale
methods.motionPurpose: Expose computational cost without merging it into the reference-error operation.
methods.stepDetail: Retain each system size; place its measured solve time; connect timings in refinement order; mark the reported endpoints.
methods.transitionBridge: The verified accuracy-cost tradeoff returns to Kinmen where the circular comparator needs an equivalent radius.

methods.input: Kinmen shoreline geometry and the circular-island analytical comparator
methods.output: Least-squares equivalent radius for the Kurylyk island solution
methods.validity: The source determines a circle that approximates Kinmen's geographic extent solely to parameterize the simplified analytical comparator while retaining the actual coastline in the image-well solution.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.6 Illustrative application, paragraph describing the Kurylyk et al. comparison
methods.sourceExcerpt: herein determined using a least-squares method to approximate
methods.visualObject: A trial circle resizing over the Kinmen shoreline until its geographic mismatch reaches the least-squares fit
methods.motionPurpose: Own the geometry reduction used by the classical comparator without replacing the irregular boundary in the proposed model.
methods.stepDetail: Retain the Kinmen outline; center a trial circle; adjust its radius against shoreline mismatch; preserve the fitted radius as comparator input.
methods.transitionBridge: The fitted circle remains a comparator while the actual irregular coastline carries the constrained pumping problem.

methods.input: Nine well locations with bounded pumping rates and a minimum interface-depth constraint
methods.validity: SLSQP handles the continuous pumping variables and inequality constraint, with each rate bounded from 0 to 1000 m3/d.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.7 Pumping optimization exercise, Equation 14
methods.sourceExcerpt: Python, with the Sequential Least Squares Programming (SLSQP) algorithm accessed
methods.visualObject: Nine well-rate columns adjusting while interface markers stay below the screen-depth limit
methods.motionPurpose: Raise total extraction until the first interface constraint becomes active, then redistribute rates among wells.
methods.stepDetail: Initialize nine rates; evaluate interface depth at each well; enforce bounds and depth constraints; update rates to maximize total pumping.
methods.transitionBridge: The optimized rates remain attached to their positions on the original island map.

return.visualObject: Kinmen Island's actual coastline with head contours, the freshwater-lens cross-section, vulnerability and reliability overlays, and nine optimized pumping rates
return.claim: Enforcing ocean head on an irregular coastline produces management-relevant head and interface geometry while retaining analytical speed for vulnerability analysis and constrained multiwell pumping.
return.claimBoundary: This is a preliminary sharp-interface assessment for homogeneous isotropic Dupuit flow with constant mean thickness, steady recharge, no tides, and no explicit transition zone; the Kinmen pumping configuration is hypothetical and illustrative.
return.locator: PDF page 8, Section 4 Conclusions; PDF page 9, concluding paragraphs
return.sourceExcerpt: solution for a simplified case, confirming that the method effectively

visual.primaryObject: Irregular island coastline enclosing a sharp-interface freshwater lens
visual.accentObject: Boundary-node image wells and interface-depth constraint markers
visual.measuredQuantity: Hydraulic head, freshwater-saltwater interface depth, vulnerability, reliability, and pumping rate
visual.geometryNotes: Preserve the island boundary while moving between plan view and cross-section with an explicit camera rotation; sample the shoreline into nodes, show image-well residuals collapsing, transform head contours into interface depth, and keep optimized well rates collocated with the original wells.
